Lam, Kar Mun
Nouri, Alireza
Mahmoudi, Ebrahim
Ang, Wei Lun
Lau, Woei Jye
Mohammad, Abdul Wahab
coredata.dc:description: Thin film composite (TFC) membrane performance is often restricted by permeability and selectivity trade-off effect, even with the modification of nanoparticles. Graphene oxide-poly(2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate) (GO-PDMAEMA) with brush structure has abundant hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups and amine groups. It was synthesized via the atom transfer radical polymerization method and incorporated into TFC polyamide layer using interfacial polymerization. The GO-PDMAEMA-modified thin film nanocomposite membranes (TFNG-P) with Turing structure and higher surface hydrophilicity enhanced water permeability by 40.7–55%, with relatively low nanoparticle loading (0.005–0.02 wt%). Besides, TFNG-P2 (0.01 wt% GO-PDMAEMA) has the highest Na2SO4 rejection (97.8%), 4.4% higher than TFC. The PDMAEMA brushes on GO with additional amine groups promoted the amide linkage and increased the PA layer cross-linking by 16.2%. The higher cross-linking did not negatively affect the membrane flux, as the better hydrophilicity offset this effect. The utilization of the modified membranes in the treatment of palm oil mill effluent demonstrated enhanced resistance to fouling with TFNG-P2, which exhibited the highest flux recovery rate, reaching 89.4%. Thus, this study showed GO modification by grafting PDMAEMA brushes had enhanced the membrane properties, resulting in TFNG-P with higher permeability, promising separation and antifouling performance.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thin film composite (TFC) membrane performance is often restricted by permeability and selectivity trade-off effect, even with the modification of nanoparticles. Graphene oxide-poly(2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate) (GO-PDMAEMA) with brush structure has abundant hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups and amine groups. It was synthesized via the atom transfer radical polymerization method and incorporated into TFC polyamide layer using interfacial polymerization. The GO-PDMAEMA-modified thin film nanocomposite membranes (TFNG-P) with Turing structure and higher surface hydrophilicity enhanced water permeability by 40.7–55%, with relatively low nanoparticle loading (0.005–0.02 wt%). Besides, TFNG-P2 (0.01 wt% GO-PDMAEMA) has the highest Na2SO4 rejection (97.8%), 4.4% higher than TFC. The PDMAEMA brushes on GO with additional amine groups promoted the amide linkage and increased the PA layer cross-linking by 16.2%. The higher cross-linking did not negatively affect the membrane flux, as the better hydrophilicity offset this effect. The utilization of the modified membranes in the treatment of palm oil mill effluent demonstrated enhanced resistance to fouling with TFNG-P2, which exhibited the highest flux recovery rate, reaching 89.4%. Thus, this study showed GO modification by grafting PDMAEMA brushes had enhanced the membrane properties, resulting in TFNG-P with higher permeability, promising separation and antifouling performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water scarcity, a growing global concern, has presented a severe threat to the well-being of communities worldwide. As the world's population expands and industrialization advances, the demand for freshwater also increases exponentially while unsustainable development depletes natural water supplies [1]. Thus, water and wastewater remediation and treatment must be prioritized to guarantee sustainable water use and to mitigate the consequences of water shortage. Membrane separation has been extensively researched in water and wastewater treatment applications. It is a technology with no phase change, low chemical use, high efficiency, and requires less energy than thermal-based treatment technology [2,3]. Nanofiltration (NF) is a promising membrane technology equipped with similar characteristics to reverse osmosis (RO), capable of selectively retaining ions, especially divalent ions, while possessing higher flux and lower energy consumption. Over the years, research has advanced in adding a selective layer on top of a support layer (substrate) to form thin film composite (TFC) membranes [4,5]. The polyamide (PA) active layer is produced when an aqueous and organic monomer interacts during the interfacial polymerization (IP) procedure. However, water permeability, rejection ability, and fouling propensity often have a trade-off effect that limits TFC membrane performance.

Countless efforts have been made to investigate techniques for modifying and enhancing membrane properties to lessen the impacts or, in the best case, overcome the membrane performance limitations. One of the widely explored and effective methods is the incorporation of nanoparticles in the membrane. Emerging nanoparticles that have grabbed the attention of researchers include metal-organic frameworks (MOF) [6], carbon nanotube (CNT) [7], Mxenes [8], graphene oxide (GO) [9] and graphene quantum dots (GQD) [10]. Being one of the emerging carbon-based nanomaterials, GO has been studied and employed in different fields, including membrane fabrication. In the case of PA TFC membrane, GO is often incorporated into the aqueous phase due to its availability of oxygen-containing functional groups, including carboxyl, hydroxyl and epoxy [11]. The hydrophilic nature of GO has also been proven to improve the performance of thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes, especially enhancement in water permeability. However, loading of GO has also been found to cause severe aggregation or defect in the PA layer. Significant drops in water permeability and the trade-off effect was observed in these cases. Zhao et al. [12] reported that when low nanoparticle loading was employed (0.005–0.01 wt%), the water flux was enhanced by 7.4–31.7%, but the salt rejection was compromised and reduced. When GO loading increased to 0.015 wt%, the water flux dropped by 26.1%. This was due to the aggregation of GO. Hence, GO modification is considered one of the main priorities for fabricating GO-based TFN membranes to reduce GO aggregation, improve the PA layer cross-linking, and mitigate the trade-off effect.

Polymer can be a promising modifier for GO as polymer usually consists of a long chain or branched structure, which increases the exposed surface area. Based on a review done by Ang et al. [13], other than as a modifier for other nanoparticles, polymers, such as polyamidoamine (PAMAM) and hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (HPEI), have also been used in modifying the PA layer structure. Such modification has improved the membrane surface properties while acting as a stabilizer to reduce the aggregation effect of nanoparticles. Aside from that, polymers having brush topologies have also shown promise in improving membrane water flow and separation performance. Keating et al. [14] have reviewed using different polymer brushes to modify the TFC PA layer surface. In short, the grafting of polymer brush like poly-(methacrylic acid) (PMAA) and poly(acrylamide) (PAAm) on top of TFC PA has enhanced the membrane water permeance, rejection rate and fouling resistance. Besides the hydrophilic brush-topology that increased the water uptaking rate, the "R" side group has also played a role. The "R" group is known as the non-polymeric side group that is often present in polymer with brush structure, and this "R" side group usually gives extra chemical functionalities to the polymer brushes [15]. Hence, polymer brushes can be engineered to enhance the membrane performance by choosing the right candidate (monomer) with the desired "R" side group.

One of the promising candidates is poly(2-(dimethylamino)ethyl acrylate) (PDMAEMA). It is a long-chain polymer that can grafted or attached to a surface or nanoparticles as polymer brushes [16]. PDMAEMA brush possesses abundant hydrophilic amine groups on its "R" side group, which can potentially participate in either the PA layer surface or PA matrix modification. Referring to work by Zhijuan et al. [17], PDMAEMA was employed in TFN membrane fabrication. Instead of directly incorporating PDMAEMA in the PA layer, the authors used DMAEMA monomer (8 g) and grafted it onto the PA layer for membrane surface modification. The results showed improved water permeability (2.6 times) while achieving comparable divalent salt rejection (∼94%) as compared to unmodified TFC membrane (∼96%). This improvement was because of the extra hydrophilic amine groups on the polymeric chain and nano-Turing structure formed that increased the surface area and enhanced the water permeance and rejection. Hence, incorporating PDMAEMA brush into the PA matrix would be promising and has yet to be explored.

Therefore, this work proposed using GO-PDMAEMA to modify the TFN membrane. PDMAEMA brush was grafted on GO sheets before adding them into an aqueous phase monomer for IP reaction. To the best of our knowledge, for the first time, a novel study on the effect of GO grafted with PDMAEMA brush incorporation into the PA layer on the NF performance will be reported in this work. It is important to note that the loading concentration in this work was kept below 0.020 wt% (0.005–0.020 wt%) to investigate the effectiveness of GO-PDMAEMA addition on performance enhancement while minimizing the use of nanoparticles. Other than unmodified TFC, GO-incorporated TFN (TFNG) membrane was fabricated as an additional control set for performance comparison and evaluation of the effect on performance and role of PDMAEMA brush in GO-PDMAEMA-incorporated TFN (TFNG-P) membrane. All the nanoparticles synthesized and membranes fabricated were analyzed with different tests to help understand their surface morphology and properties. Besides, tests for water permeability and salt rejection (Na2SO4 and NaCl) were conducted to assess membranes' performance. On top of that, as a sample of real wastewater, palm oil mill effluent (POME) was used to examine how various fabricated membranes perform against fouling in extreme environments. Lastly, the formation mechanism of the PA layer and comparison with previous PA TFN membranes were discussed at the end of the results and discussion section to help visualize the effect of PDMAEMA brush-grafted GO during the PA layer synthesis.

Natural graphite powder, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) with Mw of 200, 400, 600 and 1000 Da were purchased from Merck, Germany. Sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 95–97%), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), tetrahydrofuran (THF), sodium sulphate (Na2SO4) and potassium iodide (KI) were acquired by R&M, Malaysia. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), piperazine (PIP), n-Hexane (99%) and NaCl were from SYSTERM, Malaysia. Sigma-Aldrich, USA provided 2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate (DMAEMA) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC). Triethylamine (TEA) was from Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA, while 2-bromo-2-methylpropionyl bromide (BIBB), copper(I) bromide (CuBr) and pentamethyldiethylenetriamine (PMDETA) were from Acros Organic, USA. Both hexane and methanol were purchased from J.T.Baker, Philipsburg, New Jersey. Commercial UF polysulfone (PSF) 20 kDa (US020) was purchased from Rising Sun Membrane Technology Co., Ltd, China for membrane fabrication and performance testing. Barium chloride 2-hydrate (BaCl2) and Iodine pearl (I2) were supplied by HmbG Chemicals and Friendemann Schmidt Chemicals, Malaysia, respectively.

In this work, GO was prepared via the modified Hummer's method by using graphite powder as the precursor [18]. GO-PDMAEMA was synthesized by atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) method adapted from work done by Mogha et al. [19]. Firstly, a round-bottom flask containing 1 g of synthesized GO, 9 mL of TEA, and 35 mL of THF was added and immediately submerged in an ice bath. Then, 25 mL of BIBB was added dropwise to the flask, controlled at 0 °C for 2-hour stirring, and left stirring at room temperature overnight. The product, GO-Br, was further purified by washing with THF, methanol, and RO water and freeze dried. Next, 1 g of GO-Br, 160 mg of CuBr, 0.2 mL of PMDETA, 1 g of DMAEMA, and 20 mL of THF were added into a round-bottom flask, degassed and then immersed into a 60 °C oil bath under nitrogen gas atmosphere for polymerization. After 4 h, the reaction was stopped by diluting the mixture with THF, and the metal salt and other impurities were washed several times with THF, RO water and ethanol. Lastly, the precipitate obtained underwent filtration and washing before being freeze dried. The schematic diagram of GO-PDMAEMA formation is shown in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P membrane
ce:section.ce:para: A commercial UF membrane (PSF20) was used as the substrate layer for preparing the TFC and TFN membrane via IP. The procedures were adapted from previous work [20]. In brief, PIP and TMC were used as the aqueous and organic phase monomers, respectively, in this study. For the TFC membrane, PIP was introduced to RO water, while TMC was added to n-hexane. Both were then given a 30-minute stir at room temperature. The UF support was clamped between a silicone rubber frame and a glass plate. Firstly, after pouring the PIP aqueous solution over the support membrane, it reacted for 10 min. The PIP aqueous solution was then extracted from the membrane, and the membrane was dried before use. Then, the organic phase monomer, TMC, was poured onto the membrane and allowed to sit for 60 s. After that, the membrane underwent heat treatment in an oven at 60 °C for 10 min. The membrane was then thoroughly washed with RO water before immersing it in RO water and storing it at 4 °C before use. In this work, TFN with GO loading is denoted as TFNG, while with GO-PDMAEMA is TFNG-P. The procedures for preparing TFNG and TFNG-P were similar to TFC, except different loading of GO or GO-PDMAEMA was introduced into the aqueous phase monomer, as shown in Table 1.

A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was used to analyze the morphology of GO and GO-PDMAEMA at 200 kV using a TECNAI G2 20 S-TWIN model. Using an X-ray diffractometer from Bruker (D8 advance) with a scanning range of 2θ = 5 to 40°, an X-ray diffraction (XRD) profile was generated to assess the crystallinity of GO and GO-PDMAEMA. Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) system was utilized to look into the chemical surface functional groups of GO and GO-PDMAEMA. The 650 to 4000 cm−1 wavelength range was used to scan each sample. The height profile of GO and GO-PDMAEMA was analyzed using an atomic force microscope (AFM, NX-10, Park Systems). For the surface elemental composition of GO and GO-PDMAEMA, an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) was used to study the unique bonding presence on GO and GO-PDMAEMA (Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos/Shimadzu).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Merlin Compact, ZEISS), the surface and cross-sectional morphologies of the prepared TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P membranes were observed. The surface roughness of the membranes was examined on 3 different spots on each membrane using AFM (NX-10, Park Systems) with a scanning area of 5 µm × 5 µm. The average roughness (Ra) and root mean square roughness (Rrms) were obtained to represent the surface roughness of the fabricated membranes. With a pH range of 3–10, an electrokinetic analyzer (Anton Paar, SurPASS™) was used to measure the surface charge of the membrane. HCl (0.05 molar) and NaOH (0.05 molar) solution were used to titrate the pH, and 1 mM of KCl solution served as the background electrolyte throughout the electrokinetic study. Static and dynamic water contact angles (WCA) were used to investigate the membrane surface hydrophilicity via the sessile drop technique using a high-speed camera with Phoenix 300 Touch (SEO Co. Ltd.). The image was taken right after a water droplet with a volume of 10 μL was dropped on the surface of each membrane sample.

PEG with molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 200, 400, 600 and 1000 Da were used to estimate the MWCO of TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P2. Each solution was made into 500 ppm and filtered through the membrane at 6 bar. The spectrophotometric titration method from previous works was used to determine the PEG concentration of the permeate obtained [21,22]. In brief, 1 mL of 5 wt% BaCl2 in 1 M HCl solution was mixed with 4 mL of PEG solution. Then, after being produced and diluted 10 times, a solution with 1.27 g I2 in 100 mL of 2 wt% KI was added to the previously mixed solution. A spectrophotometer (Spectroquant® Prove 300) was used to measure absorption at 535 nm after colour developed for 15 min at room temperature to identify the concentration of PEG. XPS examined the bonding and composition of elements present on the membrane surface. The element atomic concentrations could be utilized to determine the O/N ratio and degree of PA layer cross-linking using the formulae as shown:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Membrane pure water flux, permeability and salt rejection test
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance test for all the membrane samples was carried out using a cross-flow membrane cell (CF042, Sterlitech Corporation, USA) with an effective membrane surface area of 42 cm2. Each test was repeated 3 times to obtain the average reading. The pure water flux (PWF) at 2, 4, 6, and 8 bars was measured for 15 min each after the membrane was compressed at 9 bar until it reached stable flux. The PWF and pure water permeability (PWP) were calculated by using Eqs. (4) and (5), in which V represents permeate volume over time, t and membrane effective area, A, at a set operating pressure, ΔP.

For salt rejection evaluation, 1000 ppm of NaCl and Na2SO4 were used, which represents the mono- and divalent salt, respectively, in this study. The operating pressure was at 6 bar after being compacted at 9 bar for 30 min. The percentage of salt rejection, RNa and RNa2SO4 were obtained by applying Eq. (6), where Ci and Cf represent the initial concentration of the salt solution and final concentration of the permeate, which were recorded by using a conductivity meter (Hanna Instruments, HI8633).

Raw AT-POME from Malaysian PPNJ Palm Oil Plantations, Kahang, Johor, was employed as feed to test the membrane antifouling property and its behaviour under a harsh environment. Before the test, the membrane was pre-compacted at 9 bar for 30 min, and the operating pressure for the filtration test was conducted at 6 bar. The antifouling test included a total of 3 cycles, in which each cycle consisted of pure water filtration before fouling (PWFWi) for 30 min, AT-POME fouling filtration for 5 h (PWFF1,2,3), 10 min of RO water washing and then followed by 30 min of pure water flux after fouling (PWFW1,2,3). Table S1 shows the different parameter readings of the raw AT-POME. After the test, the permeate was collected to assess the chemical oxygen demand (COD), conductivity, colour, and total suspended solids (TSS). The removal efficiency (or rejection) for all the tests can be found in Table S2. The COD test was carried out using Hach method and allowed to react in Hach DRB200, and its reading was obtained using Hach DR3900 spectrophotometer. In contrast, the reading was obtained using Spectroquant® Prove 300, a spectrophotometer for colour and TSS. To determine each fabricated membrane's antifouling capabilities, flux recovery rate (FRRi), reversible flux decline rate (RFDR), irreversible flux decline rate (IRFDR) and flux decline rate (FDR) were calculated using the Eqs. Fig. 1(a) and (b) show the HRTEM of GO and GO-PDMAEMA. GO shows sheet-liked morphology with high transparency, indicating the synthesized GO comprises mono or a few layer sheets. For GO-PDMAEMA, a negative staining technique was employed to examine the PDMAEMA morphology. Similar to GO, GO-PDMAEMA consists of sheet-liked morphology but with a darker contrast. The darker area on top of GO sheets is the PDMAEMA brush chain. This depicts that PDMAEMA has grafted on GO. As shown in Fig. 1(c), GO shows the usual XRD pattern with a diffraction peak (001) at 10.2°, with a d-spacing of 0.87 nm. For GO-PDMAEMA, it shows a peak shift to 9° with a larger d-spacing than GO, i.e., 0.98 nm. The increase in interlayer separation is due to grafting the PDMAEMA brushes onto GO [23]. Fig. 1(d) shows the FTIR result, revealing the surface functional groups on the nanoparticles. The GO exhibits previous studies' typical results and peaks ([24,25]), in which GO consists of peaks at 3363 cm−1 (OH stretching), 1730 cm−1 (CO stretching), 1625 cm−1 (CC stretching), 1415 cm−1 (C-O-H deformation vibration), 1225 cm−1 (C-O stretching) and 1077 cm−1 (C-O-C bending). It is also shown that GO-PDMAEMA consists of similar peaks mentioned in the case of GO. Aside from that, two new peaks could also be observed at 1150 cm−1 and 1467 cm-1, indicating the stretching vibration of C-N and asymmetric bending vibration of -CH3 found in the PDMAEMA brush [26,27]. Hence, this proves the successful grafting of PDMAEMA on GO.

Other than FTIR, AFM has been analyzed to confirm the successful synthesis of GO-PDMAEMA further. Fig. 2(a) and (b) correspond to the height profiles of GO sheets and GO-PDMAEMA. The image shows the GO layer with a thickness of ∼1 nm, which is in the range of the results reported by other works [28,29]. For GO-PDMAEMA, the thickness has increased to 4.3 nm after the polymer grafting. This increment in thickness signifies the grafting of the PDMAEMA chain on GO [30]. To further confirm the surface functionalization of GO sheets, XPS was conducted. Fig. 2(c) shows the survey scan of GO and GO-PDMAEMA on elements C, N, and O. The presence of N at the binding energy of 398 eV has demonstrated the existence of PDMAEMA. In addition, based on the N1s (Fig. 2(d)) spectra that appeared on GO-PDMAEMA, and the C-N peak found at 285.3 eV on GO-PDMAEMA C1s spectra as shown in Fig. S2 proved the presence of PDMAEMA on GO. Besides, from Fig. S2, the increase in the area of O-CO at 288.5 eV, from 7.7% (GO) to 9.6% (GO-PDMAEMA), has also been a vital piece of evidence of PDMAEMA brush grafting on GO nanosheet [16,31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: TFC and TFN membrane surface morphology and chemistry
ce:section.ce:para: Loading of GO and GO-PDMAEMA in the aqueous phase monomer is expected to affect the physiochemical properties of the PA layer formed during the IP process as supported by the observation from FESEM and AFM results. Fig. 3 and Fig. S3 show the TFNG and TFNG-P membrane changes regarding their surface, cross-section and surface roughness compared to the TFC membrane. TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P1 surface was covered with spherical nodules. For the case of GO-incorporated TFN, the selective layer thickness is reduced and has a smoother surface than TFC. This is because hydrophilic nanoparticles, like GO, are capable of attracting PIP and causing PIP to be absorbed in the substrate layer during IP. Previous works have reported similar observations that incorporated hydrophilic nanoparticles in PA and caused a thinner layer to form [32,33].

The incorporation of GO-PDMAEMA, on the other hand, has increased the thickness and surface roughness of the PA active layer compared to TFC. An interesting observation is that when the concentration increased (specifically when the concentration exceeded 0.005 wt%), nano-stripe Turing structure became visible, as supported by the FESEM images illustrated in Fig. 3(a) and Fig. S3(a). This structure usually forms when an interfacial interaction between the activator (aqueous monomer) and inhibitor (organic phase monomer) reacts and diffuses through the reaction-diffusion process [34]. The shape of the Turing structure can be manipulated by controlling the interfacial process [35]. In this work, PDMAEMA brush-grafted GO played a role in interfering the IP reaction, which has turned the surface of the TFN into a nano-stripe Turing structure with greater surface area than the spotted structure. Hence, this also explains the increment of Ra and RRMS of all TFNG-P from 29.5 to 77.74 nm and 36.61 to 100.79 nm, respectively. In addition, the PA layer thickness of TFNG-P membrane has also increased from 120 to 138.1 nm. These findings are similar to the work done by Xiao et al. [36] and Zhang et al. [37], who incorporated PVP-MOF and hyperbranched polyester acyl chloride (HPE-COCl) in their PA layer. These works proved that instead of causing water channelling resistance, the thicker PA layer formed can create additional channels for water transportation and storage. The thicker and rougher surface formed by GO-PDMAEMA incorporated TFN membranes in this work is due to the change in diffusion rate of the monomers (PIP and TMC) to the interface, in which the presence of GO-PDMAEMA might impact as a hindrance. PDMAEMA brush grafted-GO changed the reaction kinetics due to the monomers' diffusion route lengthening, resulting in extensive polymerization and extended contact time at the interface. Therefore, under this circumstance, the formation of a thicker PA layer could be observed. Other than PIP, PDMAEMA, which grew on GO, is a chained-polymer brush with abundant amine groups. This will trap the PIP monomers at the reaction interface and enable more PIP to engage in the IP reaction with TMC acyl chloride, and producing a thicker and denser active layer. The combination effects of affecting the IP reaction kinetic and additional interaction with the organic phase TMC by adding GO-PDMAEMA has caused the reaction system to encounter the necessary discrepancies between the PIP and TMC diffusion coefficients, causing a diffusion-driven instability and producing a rougher wrinkled-like nano-stripe Turing structure [34,38].

For all the TFNG-P membranes fabricated, only the membrane with the highest PWP and salt rejection will be chosen for selected analyses (XPS, PEG rejection, dynamic WCA and zeta potential). XPS for the survey and narrow scan spectra (Fig. S4) on C, N and O elements have been conducted to investigate the chemical composition and bonding of different membranes produced in this work. Fig. 4(a) shows the survey scan spectra of TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P2 and their respective element composition (wt%), C, N and O. TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P2 showed the anticipated C, N and O components at the 284, 398 and 530 mV peaks, respectively. From Fig. 4(a), the cross-linking degree of TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P2 membrane, which was calculated based on the Eqs. (1)-(3) in the Methodology section, shows TFNG-P2 has the highest cross-linking degree followed by TFNG and TFC. The oxygen-containing functional groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl) found on GO react with the TMC acyl chloride group to form ester bonding. At the same time, PIP interacts with amine groups to form amide bonding [39,40]. In addition, the abundance of amine groups found on PDMAEMA brushes can also react with acyl chloride to form additional amide bonds. Hence, the O content was reduced due to the utilization of the oxygen-functional groups during the IP reaction.

In many works, the O/N ratio is commonly calculated and used to indicate the TFN membrane cross-linking degree [41–43]. In contrast, there were also studies that reported these additional O and N groups found on nanoparticles can affect and interfere with the determination of PA layer cross-linking degree via O/N ratio [44,45]. Hence, the O and N groups found on the TFN membranes during the XPS analysis were attributed to two factors: the increased cross-linking of the PA layer, and the O and N groups found on the nanoparticles employed, especially in the case of utilization of high nanoparticles loading. However, in this study, the loading concentration was relatively low and kept to a maximum of 0.020 wt%. Hence, the interference to the results obtained from XPS mentioned above by O and N groups found on the nanoparticles GO and GO-PDMAEMA was minimized [20]. Sun & Wu [44] conducted PEG rejection test in their work to support further the cross-linking degree results obtained from XPS. Similar to their study, the outcome of the PEG rejection test in this work was aligned with XPS results. Based on Fig. 4(b), TFNG-P2 has the lowest MWCO, followed by TFNG and TFC. This shows that PDMAEMA has played a vital role in IP reaction. PDMAEMA brush with abundant amine groups further encourages amide linkage formation during IP, forming TFN membrane with a denser PA layer and higher cross-linking degree. Similar findings were observed by Gholami et al. [46] and Gumbi et al. [47]. The former introduced excess amine groups by adding an aromatic amine-functionalized multiwalled CNT into the PA matrix, while the latter incorporated 2,4-diaminobenzene sulfonic acid to enhance the PA cross-linking.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TFC and TFN membrane surface charge and hydrophilicity
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5(a) shows the membrane dynamic zeta potential of TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P2, which was carried out to determine the membrane surface charge. The isoelectric point where the membranes carry a zero net charge for TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P is at pH 5.6, 4.8 and 5, respectively. Besides, all the membranes showed negative surface charge at neutral and alkaline conditions, with TFNG showing the highest surface negativity, followed by TFC and TFNG-P2. In line with the earlier research that used hydrophilic nanoparticles like GO (by Lai et al. [48] and Kang et al. [33]), the incorporation of GO in this study has also further increased membrane surface negativity owing to the oxygen-containing functional groups and ionization of carboxyl and amine groups after IP. However, in this work, by growing amine-rich polymer brushes like PDMAEMA on GO, TFNG-P2 membrane surface negativity is reduced. This is attributed to the consumption of the -COO groups by amine groups during the IP reaction, lowering the surface negativity. Despite that, TFNG-P2 maintained its negative surface charge comparable with TFC.

As seen from Fig. 5(b), introducing GO and GO-PDMAEMA into an aqueous phase monomer has greatly reduced the WCA from 39° (TFC) to 28.3–31.6°. It is known that WCA is affected by both the surface topography and chemistry of the fabricated membrane. GO has been known as a hydrophilic nanoparticle with different oxygen-containing functional groups, has improved the surface hydrophilicity and decreased the WCA of TFNG. This finding is similar to previous work that incorporated hydrophilic GO-SiO2 nanoparticles and caused membrane surface hydrophilicity to increase [49]. TFNG-P, which has a rougher membrane surface than TFC and TFNG, has further improved the membrane surface hydrophilicity, which obeys Wenzel's law. According to Wenzel, for the case of non-flat membranes with hydrophilic surfaces less than 90°, the WCA decreases as the surface roughness rises [50]. The decrease in WCA can be due to a few reasons. Besides having the beneficial properties of GO, the addition of polymer brush, PDMAEMA, has provided additional hydrophilic amine groups on its chain to the PA layer. The formation of a rough nano-stripe surface and GO with polymer brushes in the PA layer have also increased the total surface area, thus creating more water storage area and water travelling path for TFNG-P membrane. The dynamic WCA was conducted for 60 s on TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P2, as illustrated in Fig. 5(c), further proving the water uptake capability of the TFNG-P2 membrane fabricated. Compared to TFC and TFNG, TFNG-P2 had the lowest static WCA and the most significant dynamic WCA reduction, within a minute, from 28.4° to 23.7°. On the other hand, when the amount of GO-PDMAEMA increased to 0.015 and 0.020 wt%, the static WCA slightly increased. This outcome can be due to the agglomerates forming in the PA layer, which caused the nanovoids to become smaller and reducing the water uptake [8]. Nevertheless, the mentioned effect does not bring a negative impact as the WCA of TFNG-P3 and TFNG-P4 are significantly lower than TFC membrane. Thus, with the incorporation of GO-PDMAEMA, TFNG-P has shown excellent surface hydrophilicity, which favour the NF membrane filtration performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NF Performance of TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P
Pure water permeability and salt rejection test
ce:section.ce:para: Previous sections have shown the beneficial effects of GO modification by using PDMAEMA on the physical and chemical properties of the fabricated NF membranes. These properties will consequently affect the NF membrane performance. In Fig. 6(a), the PWF increased as the operating pressure increased for all the fabricated membranes. Incorporating nanoparticles like GO and GO-PDMAEMA has significantly increased the PWF and PWP compared to pristine NF membranes, with TFNG-P overall showing the highest PWF and PWP, followed by TFNG and TFC. GO has increased the PWP of TFNG by 35.5% due to the thin PA layer formed and the increased surface hydrophilicity by the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups. In comparison, GO-PDMAEMA has further increased PWP of TFNG-P by 40.7–55% compared to TFC, with TFNG-P2 having the highest PWP among all the membranes fabricated. It is worth noting that PDMAEMA played a vital role in the IP reaction system for forming the PA layer. This significant improvement is attributed to the improved surface morphology and topology shown in the previous section, where the addition of GO-PDMAEMA brush has resulted in the formation of nano-stripe Turing structure and a rougher membrane surface. This structure has proven to improve the membrane water uptaking and transportation ability and is reflected through the low static and dynamic WCA obtained. Besides, as mentioned earlier, polymer brush like PDMAEMA has a unique structure with chains that can help to create additional water transportation channels and storage areas [17]. These polymer brushes on GO have once again proven to help enhance the water filtration performance by improving the membrane surface properties. The PWF and PWP decreased when the loading concentration rose to 0.015 and 0.020 wt%. Agglomeration can be the reason that caused the nanovoids formed to be compressed to a smaller size, which in turn caused the PWF and PWP to reduce. Nevertheless, the drop is marginal as the overall PWF and PWP trend is higher than the TFC membrane and all the results are aligned with FESEM, AFM and WCA.

It is known that high PWP often comes with reduced salt rejection due to the trade-off effect [35]. In this work, the fabricated TFN membranes have successfully overcome the trade-off effect, especially the TFN membranes with GO-PDMAEMA as an additive. The Na2SO4 rejection overall is higher than NaCl for all membranes, as shown in Fig. 6(b). This outcome is due to the Donnan exclusion effect and sieving mechanism, which favour the repelling and removing higher valent ions and ions with higher hydrated radii like Na2SO4. Thus, Na2SO4 is easier to retain on the membrane surface than NaCl. These results are similar to those obtained by Shen et al. [51] and Shen et al. [52]. Besides, this work showed that TFNG-P2 has the highest Na2SO4 rejection, followed by TFNG and TFC. The excellent salt rejection portraited by the TFNG-P2 membrane is primarily because of the increased PA cross-linking degree and the negatively charged membrane surface. Besides creating additional water-flowing pathways, GO-PDMAEMA has abundant amine groups on the polymer brush that can participate in IP reaction and thus form a more cross-linked layer. While for monovalent salt, NaCl rejection, all the TFNG-P membranes have also shown comparable rejection ability as TFC membrane. Overall, it is worth highlighting that the polymer brushes of GO-PDMAEMA in TFN shows promising properties that eventually led to the PWP enhancement without compromising the salt rejection ability.

Aerobically treated-POME has been seen as one of the harshest environments and liquid waste generated by the palm oil mill industry, consisting of different components like humic acid, lignin, tannin, lipids and fatty acid [53]. Fig. 6(c) and (d) show the antifouling performance of TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P2, whereas the removal efficiency of NF membranes for the selected parameters can be found in Table S2. Both TFNG and TFNG-P2 showed improved removal efficiency, especially TFNG-P2. This finding is due to improved membrane surface properties and PA layer cross-linking by GO-PDMAEMA with polymer brushes. In addition, TFNG-P2 showed the highest FRR3 (89.4%) and the lowest FDR (65.6%) compared to TFNG and TFC. Abbas et al. [54] have grafted poly(acrylic acid) brushes on top of the PA layer and found that the brushes aided the water flow across the membrane. This increased the hydrophilicity and hydration degree of the membrane structure and encouraged the formation of a hydration protective layer on top of PA layer against the attachment of pollutants. Similar results can also be obtained from the work by Liu et al. [55] in which monomethoxy-poly(ethylene glycol) brushes were grafted on top of the PA layer. Their work similarly reported that the brush structure improved the membrane hydrophilicity and prevented the foulants deposition, followed by 15% improvement in FRR for the textile effluent fouling test.

Although the work mentioned showing the case where polymer with brush structure was grafted on top of PA layer instead of adding them into the PA matrix, similar mechanisms and beneficial features offered by these polymer brushes can be portrayed in this study by incorporating PDMAEMA brush grafted-GO into PA matrix. The brushes on GO-PDMAEMA in PA matrix have increased the membrane surface hydrophilicity and, together with the rougher nano-stripe structure formed on the membrane surface, have enhanced the antifouling ability. It is known that a rougher and more hydrophilic membrane surface will encourage the formation of a hydration layer on top of the membrane surface [56,57]. As mentioned, this hydration layer aids in repelling the foulant, reducing the chance of foulant attachment on top of the membrane surface and, simultaneously, delaying the flux decline. The obtained FESEM pictures confirm these findings after 3 cycles of AT-POME fouling process. Together with the capability of repelling foulant, the high surface hydrophilicity has also eased the washing process by using only simple hydraulic washing. The washing procedure after each cycle is deemed to recover the membrane performance by removing the loosely attached foulant on the membrane surface. From the FESEM images in Fig. 7, even after conducting the antifouling test in such extreme environment, TFNG-P2 has shown the least PA layer thickness increment, 57.8%, compared to TFC and TFNG. These results suggest that the organic foulant is less likely to adhere permanently to the TFNG-P2 membrane surface. As explained in the previous section, PDMAEMA brushes grown on GO have been found to increase the surface roughness and hydrophilicity. These factors encourage water adhesion on the membrane surface, which could help rehydrate the foulant and make it easier to remove during washing. Thus, this antifouling test has shown that incorporating GO-PDMAEMA could be a promising method for enhancing the membrane antifouling capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of GO-PDMAEMA, proposed formation mechanism of PA layer on TFNG-P membrane and comparison with previous works
ce:section.ce:para: This work synthesized GO-PDMAEMA by growing PDMAEMA brushes on GO sheets. GO acts as a support material, and additionally, it possesses oxygen-containing functional groups that may aid in enhancing the hydrophilicity of the membrane. Like GO, the PDMAEMA brushes enhance membrane hydrophilicity with the hydrophilic amine groups on its chain. The addition of GO-PDMAEMA into the PA matrix has affected both PIP in the aqueous phase and TMC in the organic phase. Fig. 8 shows the simple mechanism of the formation of PA layer. Firstly, upon the addition of GO-PDMAEMA and aqueous monomer onto the substrate layer, adsorption of both GO-PDMAEMAE and PIP will occur via hydrogen bonding and also the ionic bonding between amine groups (positively charge) of GO-PDMAEMA and PIP, with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups (negatively charge) found on PSF substrate layer. Other work has also proven that the assistance of hydrophilic nanoparticles will help better adhesion of PIP on PSF substrate surface [57]. In the case of this work, with the help of GO-PDMAEMA, which has created a larger surface area and reaction sites due to the polymer brushes, it allows more PIP to interact with GO-PDMAEMA via hydrogen bonding. It simultaneously enables more PIP and GO-PDMAEMA to adsorb onto the PSF substrate. This interaction between GO-PDMAEMA and PIP with the substrate layer is essential, as it will hold both the nanoparticles and aqueous monomer in place and put them into contact at the interface between the organic and aqueous phases for IP reaction [58].

Next, the PIP and GO-PDMAEMA will react with TMC in the organic phase. Typically, the IP process is a rapid reaction [59]. PIP will diffuse into the organic phase to react with TMC to form cross-linking in this process. With the incorporation of GO-PDMAEMA, extra amine groups on the PDMAEMA brushes interact with acyl chlorides of TMC to form additional amide linkage and subsequently increase the cross-linking of the PA layer [60]. Due to the increased amine groups presence, the polymerization reaction would occur more extensively, leading to a thicker PA layer. Besides, as mentioned, the GO-PDMAEMA can interact with and entrapped PIP. Consequently, the diffusion of PIP into the organic phase extended. Nevertheless, the thicker PA layer did not negatively affect the water flux. In contrast, it enhanced the water flux by providing additional water pathways due to the addition of PDMAEMA brushes grafted on GO into the PA matrix.

Fig. 9 and Table S3 show the PA-TFN membrane performances reported in the literature. It should be noted that only NF TFN membrane modifications that were done by incorporating polymer brush, GO-based or modified GO into PA layer were discussed and compared in this section. From Fig. 9(a) and (b), the majority of the previous works employed nanoparticles with loading concentrations of 0.5 wt% and lower and, at the same time, achieved divalent salt, Na2SO4 rejection of 90% or above. This shows the potential of using low nanoparticles (especially modified GO) loading concentration in modifying PA due to the high surface area to mass ratio of GO [61]. Despite that, there is room for improvement in producing TFN with high PWP and divalent salt rejection. As seen in Fig. 9(a) and (b), with the incorporation of GO-PDMAEMA, the TFNG-P membrane fabricated in this work shows 42% and 7% higher PWP and divalent salt rejection than the average of previous studies. It is also worth highlighting that the concentration employed in this work is one of the lowest (0.01 wt%) compared to previous works. With the incorporation of GO-PDMAEMA, the TFNG-P membrane PWP is one of the highest after TFN membranes modified with TETA-GO, PDA-PEG and hyperbranched starch functionalized GO (HGOST). Nevertheless, the TFNG-P membrane in this work achieved divalent salt rejection which was 1.4 – 32.5% higher than the previously mentioned TFN membranes and also comparable to the one with the highest divalent salt rejection, octadecylamine (ODA)-GO TFN (0.6% difference). In addition, the loading concentration used in this work is also 3–20 times lower than the one with higher PWP. Furthermore, as seen in Fig. 9(c), the TFNG-P membrane fabricated in this work was also compared with those that incorporated nanoparticles with 0.05 wt% and below. Interestingly, TFNG-P membrane stands out from other previous works despite the low loading concentration. Thus, GO with PDMAEMA brush grafting is a promising candidate in modifying PA layer of TFN membrane to overcome the permeability and salt rejection trade-off while showing excellent antifouling performance for real wastewater treatment in extreme environments.

In this work, a novel GO-PDMAEMA-incorporated TFN membrane was fabricated to explore further the effect of polymer brushes on membrane PA matrix and performance. Compared to existing works, Fig. 9 clearly shows the potential of PDMAEMA brush-grafted GO incorporation in producing TFN with enhanced NF performance without negatively impacting the rejection ability. TFNG-P is the membrane with a unique nano-stripe Turing structure surface with a rough, dense, and slightly thicker PA layer. GO-PDMAEMA in TFN membranes hinders PIP diffusion, leading to changes in reaction kinetics by elongating the diffusion route. This results in extended contact time at the interface and the formation of a thicker PA layer. With the incorporation of GO-PDMAEMA, the membrane surface hydrophilicity (WCA) was decreased by 1.9–5° and 11–14.2° compared to TFNG and TFC, respectively. In addition, all the membranes fabricated have shown negative membrane surface charge at neutral and alkaline pH. TFNG-P showed the highest PWP among all the membranes fabricated, followed by TFNG and TFC. TFNG-P has 40.7–55% enhancement compared to TFC, with TFNG-P2 having the highest PWP, while TFNG showed 35.5% improvement compared to unmodified TFC. The PA layer cross-linking degree for TFC, TFNG and TFNG-P2 has also been calculated, and the PEG rejection test on the determination of membrane MWCO further supported the results. TFNG-P2 has 54.6% cross-linking degree with MWCO of 502 Da, while TFNG and TFC showed 7.4% and 16.2% lower cross-linking degree. The salt rejection tests were aligned with the cross-linking degree obtained as TFNG-P overall has the highest salt rejection compared to TFC and TFNG. TFNG-P2 has achieved the highest Na2SO4 rejection, 97.8% and 23.4% of NaCl rejection. In addition, TFNG-P2 demonstrated excellent antifouling properties with high removal efficiency. It has the highest FRR at 89.4%, which is 18.1% and 5% higher than TFC and TFNG. Thus, it is clear that grafting the PDMAEMA brush onto GO could further improve the membrane surface properties and characteristics. These improvements have consequently enhanced the membrane PWP while reducing the negative impact of the trade-off between permeability and salt rejection, simultaneously portraying remarkable antifouling properties. Lastly, GO modification by grafting polymer brushes like PDMAEMA has emerged as a promising candidate for TFN modification in enhancing membrane performance.
